title: Anthriscus stocksiana
taxonomy: Anthriscus stocksiana is a member of the family Apiaceae, commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. This family consists of over 400 genera and 3,000 species of plants, many of which are used as food or spices. The genus Anthriscus is further divided into several species, with A. sylvestris and A. frigida being closely related to A. stocksiana. The classification of A. stocksiana is often debated among botanists, as it may be a variant of A. sylvestris or a separate species altogether. Further research is needed to determine the exact classification of this plant.
uses: Anthriscus stocksiana, also known as meadow chervil or chervil, has been traditionally used in herbal medicine for its potential health benefits. It is said to have anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues and reduce the risk of infection. The plant is also believed to have antioxidant properties, which could aid in protecting the body against free radicals and oxidative stress. Some sources suggest that meadow chervil may be used to alleviate symptoms of anxiety and insomnia due to its calming effects. Further research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of using meadow chervil for medicinal purposes.

It is believed to have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which may help in reducing the risk of chronic diseases such as heart disease and cancer. parts: Anthriscus stocksiana is a species of plant in the carrot family Apiaceae, but unfortunately, information about its specific medicinal parts is limited.
